# Kaola-Workflow Roadmap

This file mirrors active unfinished work. GitHub issues are the source of truth when available.

## Active Work

| Issue | Title | Status | Workflow Project | Next Step |
|-------|-------|--------|------------------|-----------|
| #670 | fix(scripts): locateSection fence detection uses ln.trim() vs classifier ^\s{0,3} — indented-fence decoy can win (post-#665 residual) | open | — | Surfaced by #665 review (R3) + adversary (A2). #665 aligned locateSection's fence CLOSER semantics with the classifier and fixed the same indent divergence in release.unreleasedSection (^\s{0,3} anchor), but locateSection (adaptive-schema.js:~1144/1160) still detects fences via ln.trim() (any indent) while the hash-covering classifier (classifier.js:~286) anchors at ^\s{0,3}. A 4+-space-indented backtick run (indented code per CommonMark, not a fence) is treated as a fence by locateSection, so a runtime reader can select a fenced decoy the classifier does not → plan_hash mismatch wedge risk (adversary V7 demonstrated decoy-wins). Pre-existing (byte-identical pre/post #665), out of #665 AC. Fix (S): anchor locateSection's fence match at ^\s{0,3} on the raw line; byte-identical ×4 adaptive-schema copies; add a 4-space-indented decoy regression asserting locateSection ↔ classifier.sectionBody parity. Byte-anchor class → four-chain. Full body on GitHub #670. |
| #671 | fix(scripts): task-mirror refreshTaskMirror fail-opens with a raw EISDIR stack trace on a workflow-tasks.json dir collision | open | — | Surfaced by the bundle-664-665 review (R4). test-adaptive-node.js's #437-lane fixture (workflow-tasks.json collides with a directory) drives refreshTaskMirror (task-mirror.js:~143) to throw EISDIR, caught by the deliberate fail-open contract — suite exits 0 but 4 raw EISDIR stack traces print to stderr. Pre-existing (task-mirror.js not in the bundle diff). The fail-open is by design, but (a) the raw stack trace is noise that can mask real failures, and (b) a production EISDIR on the tasks path silently skips the mirror. Fix (S, low): catch EISDIR/collision explicitly, emit a one-line warning instead of a stack trace, keep the fail-open; add a regression asserting no stack-trace output on the collision path. Full body on GitHub #671. |
| #672 | fix(scripts): residual porcelain-probe fail-opens deferred from #669 — leg-dirty-commit probe + worktreeDirtyState | open | — | Surfaced by the #669 review (R1, R2) and its change-gate adversary. #669 flipped ONLY the mandated plan-validator dirty-fence (plan-validator.js:3253) to fail-closed and capped every in-scope status --porcelain content site. Two OTHER porcelain empty-catch sites remain fail-open in their own consumers, both verified byte-identical pre-#669 (genuinely pre-existing, strictly improved by the cap). Site 1 (medium): adaptive-node.js:4522 synthesizeLevel leg-dirty-commit probe catches to dirty='' → a porcelain overflow/error reports the leg clean and real leg content is silently omitted from the octopus merge; the leg_omitted_from_merge guard (plan-validator.js:3181-3196) is ancestry-only and doesn't cover the working-tree probe; leg provisioning is LIVE since #542 (adaptive-node.js:7110), so live-but-low-probability merge-correctness. Fix: treat overflow/error as cannot-prove-clean → include the leg or refuse, not silently skip. Site 2 (low): claim.js:483 worktreeDirtyState catches to 'missing', conflating unprobeable with absent; consumer cmdSweepLegacyWorktrees (claim.js:3455) treats non-'dirty' as removable → a probe failure could feed a destructive worktree removal (ENOBUFS-immune since #669 but still fail-open on other errors). Fix: distinguish unprobeable from missing, fail closed before any destructive sweep. Note (cap-completeness, non-blocking): closure-audit.js:166/173 and release.js:44 carry uncapped status --porcelain content probes outside #669's site list; both catch fail-CLOSED so robustness only, cap for uniformity when convenient. Full body on GitHub #672. |

## Rules

- A separate roadmap/research session owns discovering and adding future work to GitHub issues.
- `workflow-next` fetches GitHub issues, mirrors active implementation work here, and advances one item per cycle.
- After each workflow cycle, refresh this file from issue state.
- Move completed workflow project folders to `kaola-workflow/archive/`.
- Close linked GitHub issues only after acceptance criteria pass.
